author: Athanasius of Alexandria
interpretation: Athanasius reads the hymn as describing the voluntary descent of one who was fully God. Christ did not lose his divine nature by taking the form of a servant — he added human nature to his existing divine nature. The 'emptying' is not a subtraction of divinity but an addition of humanity. The exaltation in verses 9–11 reveals what was always true: Jesus is Lord, now publicly acknowledged.
context: Athanasius's insistence that kenosis involved no diminishment of divinity was crucial against Arian readings that used the hymn to argue the Son was less than the Father.

author: John Chrysostom
interpretation: Chrysostom reads 'being in the form of God' as an assertion of full equality with the Father — 'form' (morphe) here means the essential nature, not mere appearance. Christ's emptying consisted in concealing his glory under the disguise of a slave's condition, not in abandoning it. Chrysostom emphasizes the ethical point: if the Lord of glory humbled himself, how much more should quarrelsome Christians in Philippi humble themselves.
context: Chrysostom's moral-practical application of the hymn — humility as the antidote to church conflict — reflects its original function in Paul's letter, where it serves as the basis for an ethical exhortation.

author: Thomas Aquinas
interpretation: Aquinas reads the hymn as a compressed Christology. The pre-existent Son possessed the divine nature fully and equally with the Father. The 'emptying' was the assumption of a nature subject to suffering and death — not a change in the divine nature but the taking on of a second nature in which humiliation was possible. The exaltation applies to the human nature, which is glorified after the resurrection.
context: Aquinas's two-nature framework for interpreting the hymn became the standard Catholic reading and harmonized Philippians 2 with the Chalcedonian Definition's insistence that both natures remain complete and unconfused.

author: Martin Luther
interpretation: Luther reads the hymn as the ultimate expression of the theology of the cross. God reveals himself precisely in the place where human reason would never look — in weakness, suffering, and death. Christ's self-emptying is not merely an ethical example but the paradigm of how God always operates: divine power is hidden in apparent powerlessness. The exaltation vindicates what the cross accomplished.
context: Luther's theology of the cross, developed in the Heidelberg Disputation of 1518, found its christological foundation in Philippians 2. The hymn embodied his conviction that God works contrary to all human expectations.

author: John Calvin
interpretation: Calvin reads the 'emptying' as Christ voluntarily refraining from exercising his divine prerogatives — not losing them but choosing not to deploy them. The divine glory was genuinely present throughout the incarnation but was hidden from view as if behind a curtain. Calvin stresses that the hymn's primary function is ethical: believers should imitate Christ's mindset of putting others' interests above their own.
context: Calvin's 'voluntary non-use' interpretation of kenosis became the standard Reformed reading and avoided the theological risks of suggesting any real diminishment of the divine nature during the incarnation.

author: Ernst Käsemann
interpretation: Käsemann argues that the passage is a pre-Pauline liturgical hymn, not Paul's own composition. More controversially, he denies it is primarily ethical — it is not an example to imitate but a proclamation of a cosmic drama. The hymn narrates a divine invasion of the cosmos: the pre-existent one descends through the spheres of power, conquers death, and is enthroned as cosmic Lord. The ethical application is secondary to the kerygmatic declaration.
context: Käsemann's 1950 essay launched decades of scholarly debate about the hymn's origin, structure, and function. His challenge to the ethical reading forced interpreters to take the cosmic-soteriological dimensions more seriously.

author: Gordon Fee
interpretation: Fee argues that the hymn is both narrative Christology and ethical paradigm — Käsemann's either/or is a false dichotomy. Paul recites the story of Christ (pre-existence, incarnation, death, exaltation) precisely as the ground for the ethical imperative. The 'mind of Christ' is not abstract humility but the specific pattern of self-giving that defines the shape of Christian community. Fee also emphasizes that 'form of God' means Christ shared the very nature and prerogatives of God.
context: Fee's Philippians commentary is widely regarded as the definitive evangelical treatment. His both-and approach to the ethical/kerygmatic debate resolved a half-century of scholarly impasse.

author: N.T. Wright
interpretation: Wright reads the hymn as a deliberate Adam-Christ contrast. Adam, made in God's image, grasped at equality with God and fell. Christ, who truly possessed equality with God, refused to grasp and instead descended to the lowest place. The hymn narrates the reversal of Adam's failure: where Adam's grasping brought curse and death, Christ's self-emptying brought blessing and exaltation. Christ is the faithful human Adam failed to be.
context: Wright's Adam-Christology reading reinvigorated the narrative-theological approach to Philippians 2 and connected the hymn to the broader biblical storyline running from Genesis 3 to the new creation.
